---
title: "Wooldridge EACSPD -- Ch. 6: Additional single-equation topics (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

**Source.** Stata examples reproduced from
[UCLA OARC — EACSPD Chapter 6](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/examples/eacspd/econometric-analysis-of-cross-section-and-panel-data-by-jeffrey-m-wooldridgechapter-6-additional-single-equation-topics/).

**Book.** Wooldridge, J. M., *Econometric Analysis of Cross Section and Panel Data*,
MIT Press. Chapter 6: *Additional Single-Equation Topics*.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

```{julia}
mroz   = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("mroz"))     # 753 x 22
card   = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("card"))     # 3010 x 34
nls80  = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("nls80"))    # 935 x 17
injury = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("injury"))   # 7150 x 30
size(mroz), size(card), size(nls80), size(injury)
```

Stata's `predict r, res` after a `regress` — residuals aligned to the rows of
the original data frame, `missing` wherever the fit dropped the observation.

```{julia}
predict_res(m, df, y) = df[!, y] .- FixedEffectModels.predict(m, df)
```

# Example 6.1 on page 120 (mroz.dta) — the control-function / Hausman test

```{julia}
#=
use mroz, clear

reg educ exper expersq motheduc fatheduc huseduc if lwage ~=.
=#
mroz_w = mroz[.!ismissing.(mroz.lwage), :]   # the "if lwage ~=." sample, N = 428

fs61 = stata_regress(mroz_w; y = :educ,
                     x = [:exper, :expersq, :motheduc, :fatheduc, :huseduc])
```

```{julia}
#=
predict resids, res
reg lwage exper expersq educ resids
=#
mroz_w.resids = predict_res(fs61, mroz_w, :educ)

cf61 = stata_regress(mroz_w; y = :lwage, x = [:exper, :expersq, :educ, :resids])
```

The first stage reproduces exactly, and the control-function regression matches
the published table digit for digit: `educ = .0803918 (.0216362)` — numerically
the 2SLS estimate of Example 5.3 — with the endogeneity term
`resids = .047189 (.0285519)`, `t = 1.65`, `p = 0.099`. Marginal evidence of
endogeneity of schooling, exactly as Stata reports.

# Example 6.2 on page 121 (card.dta) — interaction with an endogenous regressor

```{julia}
#=
use card, clear
gen black_educ=black*educ
reg lwage educ black_educ black exper expersq smsa smsa66 south reg661 reg662 reg663 reg664 reg665 ///
	reg666 reg667 reg668
=#
card.black_educ   = card.black .* card.educ
card.black_nearc4 = card.black .* card.nearc4

ols62 = stata_regress(card; y = :lwage,
    x = [:educ, :black_educ, :black, :exper, :expersq, :smsa, :smsa66, :south,
         :reg661, :reg662, :reg663, :reg664, :reg665, :reg666, :reg667, :reg668])
```

```{julia}
#=
gen black_nearc4 = black*nearc4
reg educ exper expersq black smsa smsa66 south reg661 reg662 reg663 reg664 reg665 reg666 reg667 ///
      reg668 nearc4 black_nearc4
=#
fs62a = stata_regress(card; y = :educ,
    x = [:exper, :expersq, :black, :smsa, :smsa66, :south,
         :reg661, :reg662, :reg663, :reg664, :reg665, :reg666, :reg667, :reg668,
         :nearc4, :black_nearc4])
card.er = predict_res(fs62a, card, :educ)
```

```{julia}
#=
predict er, res
reg black_educ exper expersq black smsa smsa66 south reg661 reg662 reg663 reg664 reg665 reg666 reg667 ///
	reg668 nearc4 black_nearc4
=#
fs62b = stata_regress(card; y = :black_educ,
    x = [:exper, :expersq, :black, :smsa, :smsa66, :south,
         :reg661, :reg662, :reg663, :reg664, :reg665, :reg666, :reg667, :reg668,
         :nearc4, :black_nearc4])
card.br = predict_res(fs62b, card, :black_educ)
```

```{julia}
#=
predict br, res
reg lwage educ black_educ black exper expersq smsa smsa66 south reg661 reg662 reg663 reg664 reg665 ///
	reg666 reg667 reg668 er br
=#
cf62 = stata_regress(card; y = :lwage,
    x = [:educ, :black_educ, :black, :exper, :expersq, :smsa, :smsa66, :south,
         :reg661, :reg662, :reg663, :reg664, :reg665, :reg666, :reg667, :reg668,
         :er, :br])
```

```{julia}
#=
test er br
=#
stata_test(cf62, ["er", "br"])
```

```{julia}
#=
ivreg lwage (educ black_educ = nearc4 black_nearc4 ) black exper expersq smsa smsa66 south reg661 ///
	reg662 reg663 reg664 reg665 reg666 reg667 reg668
=#
iv62 = stata_ivregress_2sls(card, :lwage,
    [:educ, :black_educ] => [:nearc4, :black_nearc4],
    [:black, :exper, :expersq, :smsa, :smsa66, :south,
     :reg661, :reg662, :reg663, :reg664, :reg665, :reg666, :reg667, :reg668];
    robust = false, first = false)
```

All five regressions reproduce the published tables exactly. Highlights: OLS
gives `educ = .0707788 (.0037548)` and `black_educ = .0178595 (.006271)`; the
control-function regression and 2SLS give the *same* point estimates
`educ = .1273557` and `black_educ = .0109036` (as they must), and the joint test
of the two control functions gives `F(2, 2991) = 0.54`, `Prob > F = 0.5814` —
no evidence of endogeneity. The only difference between the two IV routes is the
standard errors: the control-function SEs are the (invalid) OLS ones
(`.0547317`) while 2SLS reports the correct ones (`.0569582`) — both match
Stata.

# Example 6.3 on page 123 (mroz.dta) — overidentification tests

The UCLA page uses `ivreg2`, whose default is the **asymptotic** variance
(divide by `N`, not `N-K`) and *z* statistics. `stata_ivregress_2sls` applies
the small-sample correction, so its SEs are `sqrt(N/(N-K))` times `ivreg2`'s.
Both are shown below.

```{julia}
#=
ivreg2 lwage  exper expersq  (educ= motheduc fatheduc huseduc)
=#
iv63 = stata_ivregress_2sls(mroz, :lwage,
    :educ => [:motheduc, :fatheduc, :huseduc], [:exper, :expersq];
    robust = false, first = false)
```

```{julia}
# `ivreg2`'s asymptotic (no small-sample-corrected) standard errors:
# multiply the small-sample SEs by sqrt((N-K)/N).
d63 = DataFrames.dropmissing(mroz[:, [:lwage, :educ, :exper, :expersq,
                                      :motheduc, :fatheduc, :huseduc]])
n63 = DataFrames.nrow(d63)
X63 = hcat(ones(n63), Float64.(d63.educ), Float64.(d63.exper), Float64.(d63.expersq))
Z63 = hcat(ones(n63), Float64.(d63.exper), Float64.(d63.expersq),
           Float64.(d63.motheduc), Float64.(d63.fatheduc), Float64.(d63.huseduc))
y63 = Float64.(d63.lwage)
Pz63 = Z63 * ((Z63' * Z63) \ Z63')
b63  = (X63' * Pz63 * X63) \ (X63' * Pz63 * y63)
u63  = y63 .- X63 * b63
V63  = ((u63' * u63) / n63) * LinearAlgebra.inv(X63' * Pz63 * X63)
se63 = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V63))

println("ivreg2-style (asymptotic) coefficients and standard errors")
for (nm, b, s) in zip(["_cons", "educ", "exper", "expersq"], b63, se63)
    Printf.@printf("  %-8s %12.7f %12.7f\n", nm, b, s)
end
```

```{julia}
#=
Sargan statistic (overidentification test of all instruments):           1.115
                                                   Chi-sq(2) P-val =   0.57263
=#
stata_estat_overid_2sls(mroz; depvar = :lwage, exog_vars = [:exper, :expersq],
    endog = :educ, instruments = [:motheduc, :fatheduc, :huseduc])
```

```{julia}
#=
ivreg2 lwage  exper expersq  (educ= motheduc fatheduc huseduc) , robust
=#
iv63r = stata_ivregress_2sls(mroz, :lwage,
    :educ => [:motheduc, :fatheduc, :huseduc], [:exper, :expersq];
    robust = true, first = false)
```

```{julia}
#=
Hansen J statistic (overidentification test of all instruments):         1.042
                                                   Chi-sq(2) P-val =   0.59389
=#
estat_overid(mroz, :lwage, :educ, [:motheduc, :fatheduc, :huseduc], [:exper, :expersq])
```

Coefficients match exactly. The **Sargan** statistic is `1.11504`,
`p = 0.5726` versus Stata's `1.115` / `0.57263`, and the robust **Hansen J** is
`1.04213`, `p = 0.5939` versus Stata's `1.042` / `0.59389` — both exact.
Standard errors: the hand-computed asymptotic SEs above
(`educ .0216720`, `exper .0132027`, `expersq .0003943`, `_cons .2840591`)
reproduce `ivreg2`'s published numbers exactly; `stata_ivregress_2sls` reports
the small-sample-corrected versions (`.021774`, `.0132649`, `.0003962`,
`.2853959`), which are `sqrt(428/424) = 1.004705` times as large. The same
factor relates the robust SEs (`stata_ivregress_2sls` `.0217033` vs `ivreg2`
`.0216016` for `educ`).

# Example 6.4 on page 125 (nls80.dta) — a RESET-type functional-form test

```{julia}
#=
use nls80, clear

reg lwage exper tenure married south urban black educ
=#
ols64 = stata_regress(nls80; y = :lwage,
                      x = [:exper, :tenure, :married, :south, :urban, :black, :educ])
```

```{julia}
#=
predict resid, res
predict yhat
(option xb assumed; fitted values)

gen y2=yhat^2
gen y3=yhat^3
gen y4=yhat^4
reg resid exper tenure married south urban black educ y2 y3
=#
nls80.resid = predict_res(ols64, nls80, :lwage)
nls80.yhat  = FixedEffectModels.predict(ols64, nls80)
nls80.y2 = nls80.yhat .^ 2
nls80.y3 = nls80.yhat .^ 3
nls80.y4 = nls80.yhat .^ 4

aux64 = stata_regress(nls80; y = :resid,
    x = [:exper, :tenure, :married, :south, :urban, :black, :educ, :y2, :y3])
```

```{julia}
#=
di 935*.0004
.374

di chi2tail(2, .374)
.82944374
=#
R2_64 = StatsBase.r2(aux64)
LM64  = StatsBase.nobs(aux64) * R2_64
Printf.@printf("Julia  : R2 = %.8f,  n*R2 = %.5f,  p = %.6f\n",
               R2_64, LM64, 1 - cdf(Chisq(2), LM64))
# Stata's exact R2 = ModelSS/TotalSS = .043635869 / 123.818521
R2_stata = 0.043635869 / 123.818521
Printf.@printf("Stata  : R2 = %.8f,  n*R2 = %.5f,  p = %.6f\n",
               R2_stata, 935 * R2_stata, 1 - cdf(Chisq(2), 935 * R2_stata))
Printf.@printf("Stata printed (rounded R2 = .0004): 935*.0004 = %.3f, p = %.8f\n",
               935 * 0.0004, 1 - cdf(Chisq(2), 0.374))
```

The baseline OLS regression matches exactly. The **auxiliary regression does not
match to the printed digits**, and this is genuine, not a translation error.
By construction the OLS residual is orthogonal to all seven regressors, and
`y2`, `y3` are smooth functions of a linear combination of those same
regressors, so the auxiliary design matrix is numerically near-singular: the
whole fitted vector lies in one nearly-degenerate direction whose *scale* is set
by floating-point noise. Julia's coefficients are a uniform ~0.12% rescaling of
Stata's (e.g. `exper`: Stata `-.7632267`, Julia `-.7622995`, ratio 1.00122;
`y2`: Stata `8.092523`, Julia `8.0827357`, ratio 1.00121), and every *t*
statistic in both outputs is the same `-0.55` / `0.55`. The *test* is unaffected:
Stata's exact `R²` (`.043635869/123.818521 = .00035242`) gives `n·R² = 0.3295`,
`p = 0.849`; Julia gives `n·R² = 0.3289`, `p = 0.848`. Stata's displayed
`di 935*.0004 = .374`, `p = .8294` uses the four-decimal rounded `R²`, which is
why the page's p-value differs from both exact computations.

# Example 6.5 on page 131 (injury.dta) — difference-in-differences

```{julia}
#=
use injury, clear

gen afchnge_highearn = afchnge*highearn
reg ldurat afchnge highearn afchnge_highearn if ky==1
=#
injury.afchnge_highearn = injury.afchnge .* injury.highearn
ky = injury[injury.ky .== 1, :]

did65 = stata_regress(ky; y = :ldurat,
                      x = [:afchnge, :highearn, :afchnge_highearn])
```

Exact reproduction (N = 5626, `F(3, 5622) = 39.54`, `R² = 0.0207`,
`Root MSE = 1.2692`). The difference-in-differences estimate is
`afchnge_highearn = .1906012 (.0685089)`, `t = 2.78`, `p = 0.005`: raising the
cap on weekly earnings for workers' compensation increased time out of work for
high earners by roughly 19 log points.

# Verification summary

| Example | Quantity | Stata | Julia | Match |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6.1 first stage | `huseduc` | .3752548 / .0296347 | .3752548 / .0296347 | yes |
| 6.1 first stage | R² | 0.4286 | 0.4286 | yes |
| 6.1 control fn | `educ` | .0803918 / .0216362 | .0803918 / .0216362 | yes |
| 6.1 control fn | `resids` | .047189 / .0285519 | .047189 / .0285519 | yes |
| 6.1 control fn | R² / Root MSE | 0.1622 / .66506 | 0.1622 / .66506 | yes |
| 6.2 OLS | `educ` | .0707788 / .0037548 | .0707788 / .0037548 | yes |
| 6.2 OLS | `black_educ` | .0178595 / .006271 | .0178595 / .006271 | yes |
| 6.2 OLS | `black` | -.4191076 / .0794021 | -.4191076 / .0794021 | yes |
| 6.2 first stage `educ` | `nearc4` | .3191761 / .0978211 | .3191761 / .0978211 | yes |
| 6.2 first stage `educ` | `black_nearc4` | .0029741 / .1767953 | .0029741 / .1767953 | yes |
| 6.2 first stage `black_educ` | `black_nearc4` | .874705 / .1047846 | .874705 / .1047846 | yes |
| 6.2 control fn | `educ` | .1273557 / .0547317 | .1273557 / .0547317 | yes |
| 6.2 control fn | `er`, `br` | -.0568274, .0070106 | -.0568274, .0070106 | yes |
| 6.2 | `test er br` | F(2,2991)=0.54, p=0.5814 | F=0.5425, p=0.5814 | yes |
| 6.2 2SLS | `educ` | .1273557 / .0569582 | .1273557 / .0569582 | yes |
| 6.2 2SLS | `black_educ` | .0109036 / .0403571 | .0109036 / .0403571 | yes |
| 6.2 2SLS | R² / Root MSE | 0.2435 / .38702 | 0.2435 / .3870 | yes |
| 6.3 `ivreg2` | `educ` coef | .0803918 | .0803918 | yes |
| 6.3 `ivreg2` | `educ` SE (asymptotic) | .021672 | .0216720 (hand-computed) | yes |
| 6.3 `ivreg2` | `educ` SE via StatEcon | .021672 | .0217740 | **no** — small-sample factor sqrt(428/424) |
| 6.3 `ivreg2` | `_cons` SE (asymptotic) | .2840591 | .2840591 (hand-computed) | yes |
| 6.3 | Sargan χ²(2) / p | 1.115 / 0.57263 | 1.11504 / 0.57263 | yes |
| 6.3 robust | `educ` SE | .0216016 | .0217033 | **no** — same sqrt(428/424) factor |
| 6.3 robust | Hansen J χ²(2) / p | 1.042 / 0.59389 | 1.04213 / 0.59389 | yes |
| 6.4 OLS | `educ` | .0654307 / .0062504 | .0654307 / .0062504 | yes |
| 6.4 aux. | `exper` | -.7632267 / 1.397385 | -.7622995 / 1.3971773 | **no** — see note below |
| 6.4 aux. | `y2` | 8.092523 / 14.74836 | 8.0827357 / 14.7461658 | **no** — see note below |
| 6.4 aux. | `y3` | -.4012701 / .7281559 | -.4007868 / .7280471 | **no** — see note below |
| 6.4 aux. | all *t* statistics | ±0.55 | ±0.55 | yes |
| 6.4 aux. | exact R² | .00035242 | .00035173 | ~ (5th decimal) |
| 6.4 | LM test conclusion | χ²(2) ≈ 0.33–0.37, p ≈ 0.83–0.85 | 0.3289, p = 0.848 | yes (qualitative) |
| 6.5 | `afchnge` | .0076573 / .0447173 | .0076573 / .0447173 | yes |
| 6.5 | `highearn` | .2564785 / .0474464 | .2564785 / .0474464 | yes |
| 6.5 | `afchnge_highearn` | .1906012 / .0685089 | .1906012 / .0685089 | yes |
| 6.5 | `_cons` | 1.125615 / .0307368 | 1.1256154 / .0307368 | yes |
| 6.5 | N / R² / Root MSE | 5626 / 0.0207 / 1.2692 | 5626 / 0.0207 / 1.2692 | yes |

## Mismatches and their explanations

1. **Example 6.3, standard errors from `stata_ivregress_2sls`.** The page uses
   `ivreg2`, which by default divides the residual sum of squares by `N`;
   `stata_ivregress_2sls` (like `regress`/`ivregress`) divides by `N - K`.
   The ratio is exactly `sqrt(428/424) = 1.0047053`, verified numerically
   in the notebook — the notebook also hand-computes the `ivreg2` variance and
   recovers all four published SEs to seven decimals.

2. **Example 6.4, the auxiliary regression coefficients.** The regression is
   numerically ill-conditioned: the dependent variable is the OLS residual,
   which is exactly orthogonal to all seven regressors, and `y2`/`y3` are
   polynomial transforms of the same linear index. Julia's whole coefficient
   vector is a 1.0012 rescaling of Stata's, i.e. the two solvers land on
   slightly different points along one nearly-degenerate direction. All *t*
   statistics agree (±0.55), the exact `R²` values agree to five decimals
   (.00035242 vs .00035173), and the LM test reaches the same conclusion. This
   is a floating-point conditioning difference between Stata's and
   `FixedEffectModels`'s least-squares solvers, not a specification difference.

Nothing on the Chapter 6 page was left untranslated.
